docs.title: cq-safety
docs.text:
Python dependency vulnerability scanning with Safety v2.

Safety checks your Python packages against a curated database of known CVEs, helping you catch vulnerable dependencies before they reach production.

docs.title: Why Safety?
docs.text:
A06:2021 — Vulnerable and Outdated Components

The OWASP Top 10 lists using components with known vulnerabilities as one of the most critical web application security risks. Safety automates detection of these vulnerabilities in your Python supply chain.

Feature Description 🔍 CVE scanning Checks packages against the Safety vulnerability database 📄 Requirements file scan safety check -r requirements.txt 🔧 Policy files v2 Version 2.0 schema with severity thresholds and ignore lists 🔑 API key access API key for CI/CD and commercial daily DB updates 🚀 CI/CD ready Native support for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pre-commit 📊 Multiple output formats JSON, bare, full-report, short-report

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Install Safety
pip install safety

# Scan installed packages
safety check

# Scan a requirements file
safety check -r requirements.txt

# Scan from pip freeze
pip freeze | safety check --stdin

# JSON output
safety check --json

# CI/CD scan with API key
safety check --key $SAFETY_API_KEY -r requirements.txt

docs.title: This Project
docs.text:
This repository (cq-safety) is a code-quality reference project for the Safety v2 tool. It provides:

  • A full tutorial covering installation through CI/CD integration
  • Reusable GitHub Actions and GitLab CI snippets
  • Policy file v2 templates for enterprise workflows

Use the navigation to explore the docs.

docs.title: GitHub Actions
docs.text:
No requirements.txt? Use environment scanning

If your project uses uv or pyproject.toml without a requirements.txt, omit the -r flag. Safety v2 will scan all packages installed in the active environment.

docs.title: Basic Workflow — Environment Scan (uv / pyproject.toml)
docs.text:
# .github/workflows/security.yml
name: Security Scan

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  safety:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install uv
        uses: astral-sh/setup-uv@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        run: uv python install 3.11

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: uv sync --group dev

      - name: Run Safety check
        run: |
          uv run safety check \
            --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY" \
            --policy-file .safety-policy.yml \
            --full-report \
            --save-json reports/safety-report.json
        env:
          SAFETY_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SAFETY_API_KEY }}

docs.title: Basic Workflow — Requirements File (pip / requirements.txt)
docs.text:
jobs:
  safety:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt safety

      - name: Run Safety check
        run: |
          safety check \
            --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY" \
            -r requirements.txt \
            --policy-file .safety-policy.yml \
            --full-report
        env:
          SAFETY_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SAFETY_API_KEY }}

docs.title: With JSON + HTML Artifact (uv)
docs.text:
- name: Create reports directory
        run: mkdir -p reports

      - name: Run Safety check
        run: |
          uv run safety check \
            --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY" \
            --policy-file .safety-policy.yml \
            --full-report \
            --save-json reports/safety-report.json
        env:
          SAFETY_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SAFETY_API_KEY }}

      - name: Upload Safety reports
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: safety-reports
          path: reports/
          retention-days: 90

docs.title: Scheduled Nightly Scan
docs.text:
Catch newly published CVEs against your pinned dependencies:

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * *"   # Daily at 06:00 UTC
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

docs.title: Adding the API Key Secret
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions
  2. Click New repository secret
  3. Name: SAFETY_API_KEY, Value: your pyup.io API key from pyup.io/account/api-key

docs.title: GitLab CI
docs.text:
No requirements.txt? Use environment scanning

If your project uses uv or pyproject.toml without a requirements.txt, omit the -r flag. Safety v2 scans all packages installed in the active environment.

docs.title: Basic Job — Environment Scan (uv / pyproject.toml)
docs.text:
# .gitlab-ci.yml
safety-scan:
  stage: test
  image: python:3.11-slim
  before_script:
    - pip install uv
    - uv sync --group dev
  script:
    - uv run safety check
        --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY"
        --policy-file .safety-policy.yml
        --full-report
        --save-json reports/safety-report.json
  variables:
    SAFETY_API_KEY: $SAFETY_API_KEY

docs.title: Basic Job — Requirements File (pip / requirements.txt)
docs.text:
safety-scan:
  stage: test
  image: python:3.11-slim
  script:
    - pip install safety
    - pip install -r requirements.txt
    - safety check
        --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY"
        -r requirements.txt
        --policy-file .safety-policy.yml
        --full-report
  variables:
    SAFETY_API_KEY: $SAFETY_API_KEY

docs.title: With JSON Artifact (uv)
docs.text:
safety-scan:
  stage: test
  image: python:3.11-slim
  before_script:
    - pip install uv
    - uv sync --group dev
  script:
    - mkdir -p reports
    - uv run safety check
        --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY"
        --policy-file .safety-policy.yml
        --full-report
        --save-json reports/safety-report.json
  artifacts:
    name: safety-report
    paths:
      - reports/safety-report.json
    when: always
    expire_in: 30 days
  variables:
    SAFETY_API_KEY: $SAFETY_API_KEY

docs.title: Setting the API Key Variable
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Settings → CI/CD → Variables
  2. Add variable: SAFETY_API_KEY (mark as Masked)
  3. Get your API key from pyup.io/account/api-key

docs.title: Pre-commit Hook
docs.text: Run Safety automatically before every commit to catch vulnerabilities early.

docs.title: 1. Install pre-commit
docs.text:
pip install pre-commit
# or with uv:
uv add --dev pre-commit

docs.title: 2. Add the Safety hook to .pre-commit-config.yaml
docs.text:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks-safety
    rev: v1.3.3
    hooks:
      - id: python-safety-dependencies-check
        args: ["--short-report"]

docs.title: 3. Install the hooks
docs.text: pre-commit install

docs.title: Running Manually
docs.text:
# Run Safety hook on all files
pre-commit run python-safety-dependencies-check --all-files

# Run all hooks
pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: With Policy File Args
docs.text:
Pass extra arguments to safety check via the args list:

repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks-safety
    rev: v1.3.3
    hooks:
      - id: python-safety-dependencies-check
        args: ["--policy-file", ".safety-policy.yml", "--full-report"]

docs.title: With API Key (CI environments)
docs.text:
repos:
  - repo: https://github.com/Lucas-C/pre-commit-hooks-safety
    rev: v1.3.3
    hooks:
      - id: python-safety-dependencies-check
        args: ["--key", "${SAFETY_API_KEY}", "--short-report"]

docs.title: Skipping the Hook (Emergency)
docs.text:
# Skip all hooks for one commit (use sparingly)
git commit --no-verify -m "emergency fix"

# Skip only the safety hook
SKIP=python-safety-dependencies-check git commit -m "emergency fix"

Warning

Skipping hooks bypasses your security gate. Always follow up with a tracked issue.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.7+ (3.11+ recommended)
  • pip or uv

docs.title: Install Safety v2
docs.text:
pipuv (recommended)pipx (global tool) 
pip install safety

# Add as a dev dependency
uv add --dev safety

# Sync the environment
uv sync

pipx install safety

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
safety --version
# safety, version 2.x.x

docs.title: API Key Setup
docs.text: Safety v2 uses an API key for CI/CD access and to unlock daily database updates.

docs.title: Get an API Key
docs.text:
Register for a free account and get your API key at pyup.io/account/api-key/.

  • Free tier: database updated monthly
  • Commercial tier: database updated daily

docs.title: Configure the API Key
docs.text:
# Set as environment variable (recommended)
export SAFETY_API_KEY=your-key-here

# Or pass directly on the command line
safety check --key your-key-here -r requirements.txt

Tip

Store the key as a repository secret (SAFETY_API_KEY) in GitHub or GitLab — never commit it to source control.

docs.title: No Browser Login
docs.text: Safety v2 does not use browser-based authentication. Authentication is via API key only.

docs.title: 1. Install Safety
docs.text:
pip install safety
# or with uv:
uv add --dev safety

docs.title: 2. Run safety check
docs.text:
# Scan currently installed packages
safety check

# Scan a requirements file
safety check -r requirements.txt

# Scan from pip freeze output
pip freeze | safety check --stdin

Safety checks your packages against a curated database of known CVEs.

docs.title: 3. Check a Requirements File
docs.text:
safety check -r requirements.txt

You can pass multiple files:

safety check -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt

docs.title: 5. Fix Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Upgrade the vulnerable package:

pip install --upgrade requests
# or with uv:
uv add "requests>=2.20.0"

Then re-run the scan to confirm the issue is resolved:

safety check -r requirements.txt

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Safety v2 is built around safety check as the primary command.

docs.title: safety check
docs.text:
Scan packages for known vulnerabilities.

safety check [OPTIONS]
Option Default Description -r <file> — Requirements file to check (repeatable) --stdin off Read package list from stdin --full-report off Show full advisory text --short-report off Show short one-line summary --bare off Output only vulnerable package names --json off Output results as JSON array of arrays --ignore <id> — Ignore a vulnerability ID (repeatable) --key <api-key> — API key for daily DB updates --db <path> — Use a local vulnerability database --cache off Cache the vulnerability database --policy-file <path> — Apply a local v2 policy file --proxy-host <host> — HTTP proxy hostname --proxy-port <port> — HTTP proxy port --proxy-protocol <proto> https Proxy protocol

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Scan installed packages
safety check

# Scan a requirements file
safety check -r requirements.txt

# Scan from pip freeze
pip freeze | safety check --stdin

# Full report
safety check --full-report -r requirements.txt

# CI/CD with API key and JSON output
safety check --key $SAFETY_API_KEY --json -r requirements.txt > report.json

# Ignore a CVE
safety check --ignore 36546 -r requirements.txt

# Use a policy file
safety check --policy-file .safety-policy.yml -r requirements.txt

# Use local database
safety check --db /path/to/safety-db -r requirements.txt

# Cache database for faster repeated runs
safety check --cache -r requirements.txt

docs.title: safety generate
docs.text:
# Generate a default v2 policy file
safety generate policy_file

docs.title: safety review
docs.text:
# Review a saved JSON report
safety review --file reports/safety-report.json

docs.title: safety alert (Deprecated)
docs.text:
# alert is deprecated — use Safety integrations instead
safety alert

docs.title: Commands that do NOT exist in v2
docs.text: Command Note safety scan v3 only safety auth v3 only — v2 uses API keys safety validate v3 only safety configure v3 only

docs.title: safety check-updates
docs.text:
Check if a newer version of Safety CLI is available:

safety check-updates

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text:
Safety v2 uses the following exit codes, making it easy to integrate into shell scripts and CI/CD pipelines.

Code Meaning 0 ✅ No vulnerabilities found 1 ❌ One or more vulnerabilities found 64 ⚠️ Command-line usage error 65 ⚠️ No packages found to scan 66 ⚠️ Failed to fetch the vulnerability database

Controlling what causes a non-zero exit

Use security.ignore-cvss-severity-below in your .safety-policy.yml to ignore low-severity findings. 

version: "2.0"
security:
  ignore-cvss-severity-below: 7  # Only fail on High (7+) and Critical (9+)
  ignore-cvss-unknown-severity: False
  continue-on-vulnerability-error: False

docs.title: Shell Script Example
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
set -e

safety check --key "$SAFETY_API_KEY" -r requirements.txt --policy-file .safety-policy.yml
EXIT_CODE=$?

case $EXIT_CODE in
  0)
    echo "✅ No vulnerabilities found."
    ;;
  1)
    echo "❌ Vulnerabilities detected — aborting deployment."
    exit 1
    ;;
  66)
    echo "⚠️  Could not reach Safety DB. Check network connectivity."
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

docs.title: Makefile Integration
docs.text:
.PHONY: security
security:
@safety check --key "$(SAFETY_API_KEY)" -r requirements.txt || (echo "Security check failed"; exit 1)

docs.title: GitHub Actions — Fail on Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
- name: Run Safety check
  run: safety check --key $SAFETY_API_KEY -r requirements.txt
  env:
    SAFETY_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.SAFETY_API_KEY }}
  # Step fails automatically when exit code is non-zero

docs.title: No Active Virtual Environment
docs.text:
Safety v2 scans the currently installed packages by default (no -r flag). If the wrong environment is active, you'll get incorrect results.

Ensure the correct virtual environment is activated:

source .venv/bin/activate
safety check

Or scan a requirements file explicitly:

safety check -r requirements.txt

docs.title: "Unable to load vulnerability database"
docs.text:
Safety cannot reach the remote database.

Use cached database: 

safety check --cache -r requirements.txt

Use a local database: 

safety check --db /path/to/safety-db -r requirements.txt

Check internet access: 

curl -I https://pyup.io

Use a proxy: 

safety check --proxy-host 192.168.0.1 --proxy-port 8080 -r requirements.txt

docs.title: False Positives (--ignore)
docs.text:
If Safety flags a CVE that is not exploitable in your context:

# Ignore via CLI flag
safety check --ignore 36546 -r requirements.txt

# Or add to policy file

# .safety-policy.yml
version: "2.0"
security:
  ignore-vulnerabilities:
    36546:
      reason: "Not exploitable in our deployment"
      expires: "2025-06-30"

docs.title: Slow CI Scans
docs.text:
Cache the Safety vulnerability database between runs:

safety check --cache -r requirements.txt

In GitHub Actions:

- name: Cache Safety data
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.safety
    key: safety-${{ runner.os }}-${{ hashFiles('**/requirements*.txt') }}

- name: Run Safety check
  run: safety check --cache -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Wrong Environment Being Scanned
docs.text:
Pass the requirements file explicitly to control exactly what is scanned:

safety check -r requirements.txt
safety check -r requirements-prod.txt

docs.title: API Key Not Working
docs.text:
• Verify the key is correct at pyup.io/account/api-key/
  • Ensure SAFETY_API_KEY is exported in the shell
  • Pass it directly: safety check --key your-key-here -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
safety --help
safety check --help
  • Safety v2 documentation
  • Safety GitHub Issues

docs.title: Ignoring Vulnerabilities
docs.text: Safety v2 provides two ways to ignore known false positives: the --ignore CLI flag and the policy file.

docs.title: --ignore CLI Flag
docs.text:
Pass one or more vulnerability IDs to skip:

safety check --ignore 36546 -r requirements.txt

# Ignore multiple CVEs
safety check --ignore 36546 --ignore 40291 -r requirements.txt

This is suitable for one-off overrides. For persistent, team-wide ignores, use the policy file.

docs.title: Policy File ignore-vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Generate a default policy file:

safety generate policy_file
# Creates .safety-policy.yml in the current directory

Then configure ignores under security.ignore-vulnerabilities:

# .safety-policy.yml
version: "2.0"

security:
  ignore-cvss-severity-below: 0        # 0=all, 4=medium+, 7=high+, 9=critical only
  ignore-cvss-unknown-severity: False
  ignore-vulnerabilities:
    36546:
      reason: "Not exploitable — our app does not follow HTTP redirects"
      expires: "2025-06-30"
  continue-on-vulnerability-error: False

Apply the policy:

safety check --policy-file .safety-policy.yml -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Always add a reason field for every ignored CVE in the policy file
  • Always add an expires date — re-evaluate when it passes
  • Never ignore critical severity without security team approval
  • Commit .safety-policy.yml to version control for team-wide consistency
  • Remove an ignore entry as soon as the package is upgraded past the affected version

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text: Safety v2 supports four output formats.

docs.title: Full Report
docs.text:
safety check --full-report -r requirements.txt

Includes the full advisory text for each vulnerability.

docs.title: Short Report
docs.text:
safety check --short-report -r requirements.txt

Shows a condensed one-line summary per vulnerability.

docs.title: JSON
docs.text:
safety check --json -r requirements.txt
# or save to a file
safety check --json -r requirements.txt > safety-report.json

Best for CI/CD pipelines and programmatic processing.

v2 JSON output is a list of lists. Each inner list has the structure:

[package_name, affected_range, installed_version, advisory_text, vuln_id]

Example: 

[
  [
    "requests",
    "<2.20.0",
    "2.19.1",
    "Requests before 2.20.0 sends an HTTP Authorization header to an http URI upon receiving a same-hostname https-to-http redirect...",
    "36546"
  ]
]

docs.title: Parsing in Python
docs.text:
import json
import subprocess

result = subprocess.run(
    ["safety", "check", "--json", "-r", "requirements.txt"],
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
    check=False,   # exit code 1 on vulnerabilities — handle manually
)
vulnerabilities = json.loads(result.stdout)
for vuln in vulnerabilities:
    package_name, affected_range, installed_version, advisory, vuln_id = vuln
    print(f"{package_name} {installed_version} — {vuln_id} (affected: {affected_range})")

docs.title: Bare
docs.text:
safety check --bare -r requirements.txt

Outputs only the names of vulnerable packages, one per line. Useful for piping into other tools:

safety check --bare -r requirements.txt | xargs echo "Vulnerable:"

docs.title: Safety Policy Files (v2)
docs.text: Safety v2 uses a version 2.0 policy schema to configure severity thresholds, ignored CVEs, and scan behaviour.

docs.title: Generate a Policy File
docs.text:
safety generate policy_file
# Creates .safety-policy.yml in the current directory

Review an existing JSON report:

safety review --file reports/safety-report.json

docs.title: Full v2 Policy File Reference
docs.text:
# .safety-policy.yml
# Docs: https://docs.pyup.io/docs/safety-20-policy-files
version: "2.0"

security:
  # Ignore vulnerabilities with CVSS score below this number (0–10).
  # 0  = report everything (default)
  # 4  = ignore Low; report Medium, High, Critical
  # 7  = ignore Low & Medium; report High & Critical
  # 9  = ignore all except Critical
  ignore-cvss-severity-below: 0

  # Treat vulnerabilities with unknown CVSS score as failures
  ignore-cvss-unknown-severity: False

  # Vulnerabilities to ignore — always include reason and expiry
  ignore-vulnerabilities:
    36546:
      reason: "Not exploitable — no HTTP redirects in our deployment"
      expires: "2025-06-30"
    40291:
      reason: "Mitigated by WAF — awaiting upstream patch"
      expires: "2025-09-01"

  # Set True to suppress non-zero exit codes when vulnerabilities are found.
  # Keep False in CI so pipelines fail on vulnerabilities.
  continue-on-vulnerability-error: False

docs.title: version
docs.text: Must be "2.0" for the v2 schema.

docs.title: security.ignore-cvss-severity-below
docs.text:
A numeric threshold (float, 0–10). Vulnerabilities with a CVSS score below this value are ignored.

Value Effect 0 Report everything (default) 4.0 Ignore Low; report Medium, High, Critical 7.0 Ignore Low & Medium; report High & Critical 9.0 Report Critical only

docs.title: security.ignore-cvss-unknown-severity
docs.text: False — treat unknown-CVSS vulnerabilities as failures (recommended) True — silently skip vulnerabilities with no CVSS score

docs.title: security.ignore-vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Map of Safety vulnerability IDs to ignore entries.

Field Required Description reason Recommended Why this CVE is safe to ignore expires Recommended ISO 8601 date (YYYY-MM-DD) after which the ignore should be reviewed

docs.title: security.continue-on-vulnerability-error
docs.text: False — exit with code 1 when vulnerabilities are found (recommended for CI) True — always exit 0 even when vulnerabilities are found

docs.title: Using the Policy File
docs.text:
# Apply during a scan
safety check --policy-file .safety-policy.yml -r requirements.txt

# Generate a fresh policy file
safety generate policy_file

# Review a saved JSON report
safety review --file reports/safety-report.json

Tip

Commit .safety-policy.yml to version control so all team members and CI/CD pipelines use consistent ignore rules.

docs.title: Primary Command: safety check
docs.text: safety check [OPTIONS]

docs.title: Full Flag Reference
docs.text: Flag Description -r <file> Check packages listed in a requirements file (repeatable) --stdin Read package list from stdin (e.g., pip freeze | safety check --stdin) --full-report Show full advisory text for each vulnerability --short-report Show a short, one-line summary per vulnerability --bare Output only vulnerable package names (one per line) --json Output results in JSON format (array of arrays) --ignore <id> Ignore a vulnerability by ID (repeatable) --key <api-key> API key for daily DB updates --db <path> Use a local vulnerability database directory --cache Cache the vulnerability database locally (speeds up CI) --policy-file <path> Apply a local v2 policy file --proxy-host <host> HTTP proxy hostname --proxy-port <port> HTTP proxy port --proxy-protocol <proto> Proxy protocol (default: https)

docs.title: Scan installed packages
docs.text: safety check

docs.title: Scan a requirements file
docs.text: safety check -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Scan multiple files
docs.text: safety check -r requirements.txt -r requirements-dev.txt

docs.title: Scan from pip freeze
docs.text: pip freeze | safety check --stdin

docs.title: Full report with advisory text
docs.text: safety check --full-report -r requirements.txt

docs.title: CI/CD scan with API key
docs.text: safety check --key $SAFETY_API_KEY -r requirements.txt

docs.title: JSON output
docs.text: safety check --json -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Ignore a specific CVE
docs.text: safety check --ignore 36546 -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Use a local vulnerability database
docs.text: safety check --db /path/to/safety-db -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Cache the database for faster CI
docs.text: safety check --cache -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Scan with a policy file
docs.text: safety check --policy-file .safety-policy.yml -r requirements.txt

docs.title: Using uv run
docs.text:
If your project uses uv, prefix commands with uv run:

uv run safety check -r requirements.txt
uv run safety check --key $SAFETY_API_KEY --json -r requirements.txt
